text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have spent several studies looking at the features of the Golden Altar of Incense and how each one pointed to the high priestly ministry of Christ.

We have seen the position before the veil, the continuous morning and evening burning, the sacred incense, the fire from sacrifice and prohibition of strange incense. Today we look at the final features: the two rings, the two poles, and what each of them reveals. And within the poles there is a spiritual pattern that opens up the entire structure of how Christ’s ministry is witnessed and carried both in the Old Testament and in the new creation assembly today.

Every major piece of furniture in the tabernacle the Ark, the Table of Showbread, the Bronze Altar of Burnt Offering had four rings, one at each corner. But the Golden Altar of Incense was different. It had only two rings, placed on opposite sides. This is not a minor detail. In a tabernacle where every measurement and every material was given by God with exact precision,
the difference between four rings and two rings is deliberate and speaks.

The two rings of the altar can be understood as the Law and the Prophets the two great divisions of the Old Testament that testified concerning Christ.
Christ Himself spoke of these two in Matthew 5:17: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfil them.” And again in Matthew 11:13: “For all the Prophets and the Law prophesied until John.” The entire Old Testament the Law and the Prophets was a witness pointing forward to Christ. They were not the ministry itself. They were the fixed connecting points through which the ministry of Christ was to be carried to the world. In John 5:39 Christ said to the scribes:
“You search the Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life; and it is they that bear witness about me.” And in Luke 24:27, walking with the disciples on the road to Emmaus, He “beginning with Moses and all the Prophets … interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.” The two rings, fixed permanently to the altar, are the Law and the Prophets permanent, unchanging, inseparably attached to the altar, always pointing to Christ.

Now comes the most searching question. If the two rings are the Law and the Prophets, the fixed Old Testament testimony pointing to Christ then what are the two poles? The poles are what actually moved the altar through the wilderness. The rings were fixed. The poles were what did the carrying. The rings held them in place. The poles bore the weight and walked the journey.

The two poles can be understood as the Spirit and the Truth the two powers through which the fulfilled testimony of Christ now moves through the new creation assembly. Christ Himself gave us both of these together in
John 4:23-24: “The true worshippers shall worship the Father in **spirit** and in **truth**, for the Father is seeking such people to worship Him. God is spirit, and those who worship Him must worship in **spirit and truth**.” Spirit and truth together both poles, together, carrying the altar. The Spirit carries the truth.
The truth is borne by the Spirit. The two poles work together.

Paul confirms this pattern in 2 Corinthians 3:6: “The letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.” And in Galatians 3:24: “The Law was our schoolmaster to bring us to Christ.” The Law and the Prophets the rings were the schoolmaster that brought us to Christ, fixed and permanent testimony pointing toward Him.
But the carrying forward of Christ’s ministry through the wilderness of this age is done by the Spirit and the Truth the poles inserted through those rings and borne by the new creation assembly. The picture is complete: the Altar is Christ. The rings are the Law and the Prophets testifying concerning Him.
The poles are the Spirit and Truth by which His ministry is carried through the called-together people of God into every place the cloud leads.

Here is a detail that sets the incense altar apart from the Ark of the Covenant in a significant way. When God gave Moses the instructions for the Ark, He added a specific command about its poles in Exodus 25:15: “The poles shall remain in the rings of the Ark; they shall not be taken from it.” The Ark’s poles were permanently in place, even when the Ark was at rest. But for the Golden Altar of Incense, no such command was given. The poles were inserted when the altar needed to be carried and removed when the altar was settled. They were not permanently fixed. They were present for the journey and absent when the camp was at rest.

This is deeply significant. The Ark’s poles being permanently fixed speaks of the fact that the presence of God is always ready to move, always accessible, always in a posture of going with His people. But the incense altar’s removable poles speak of something different: the Spirit and Truth do their fullest work of carrying when the people are in motion when the cloud is moving, when the assembly is actively walking the wilderness journey in obedience. When Israel was settled and at rest in the camp, the poles came out of the altar. The altar still burned. The incense still rose. But the carrying poles were set aside because the journey had paused. When the cloud lifted and the camp moved again, the poles were inserted and the carrying began. This speaks of the Spirit’s movement with an obedient, moving, willing people

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, Your word is truth (John 17:17), and Your Spirit is the Spirit of truth (John 16:13). We thank You that You did not leave us without a way to carry the ministry of Christ forward through the wilderness of this age. You gave us two rings the Law and the Prophets that testify of Your Son, standing fixed and permanent as the foundation of all witness. And You gave us two poles the Spirit and the Truth to carry that testimony into every place the cloud leads. Forgive us for the times we have tried to carry the altar without the poles relying on religious habit or tradition rather than on the Spirit and the Truth living and active in us. Teach us to know when to carry and when to rest. Teach us to follow the cloud. In Christ’s name, amen.
